title: Effects of Breathing Exercises in Patients With Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease: A Network Meta-analysis
journal: Archives of physical medicine and rehabilitation
headline: Effects of Breathing Exercises in Patients With Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease: A Network Meta-analysis
summary: A meta-analysis / systematic review of yoga examined clinical research. A network meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials (RCTs) was conducted to compare and rank the effectiveness of various breathing exercises for patients with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD).
keyFindings: A network meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials (RCTs) was conducted to compare and rank the effectiveness of various breathing exercises for patients with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD).
We searched PubMed, Web of Science, Embase, and the Cochrane Library databases to determine the articles.
limitations: Pooled estimates combine heterogeneous studies; effect sizes vary by practice, population, and control condition.

The practice studied (yoga) is not the Miracle of Mind practice, so its findings don't transfer directly — though the shared breath-regulation component may make some transfer plausible (see “Why this matters for the 7-minute practice” below).
simplifiedTitle: Effects of Breathing Exercises in Patients With Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease: A Network Meta-analysis
journalName: Archives of physical medicine and rehabilitation
aiSnippet: Meta-analysis / Systematic review, Archives of physical medicine and rehabilitation (2024). A network meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials (RCTs) was conducted to compare and rank the effectiveness of various breathing exercises for patients with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). We searched PubMed, Web of Science, Embase, and the Cochrane Library databases to determine the articles.
whyItMatters: MoM shares breath regulation with the practice studied here. Because the benefit is linked to that mechanism rather than to any one method, it's reasonable to expect it may extend to MoM as well — though MoM hasn't been studied directly for this outcome.
